questions.question_text: Which form must a Mississippi broker present to prospective clients before establishing a working relationship?
questions.correct_answer: Form A: Working with a Real Estate Broker
distractors: Form B: Listing Agreement Disclosure
Form C: Transaction Summary Notice
Form D: Brokerage Services Addendum
questions.explanation: Mississippi brokers use MREC Agency Disclosure Form A, 'Working with a Real Estate Broker,' to document that the consumer was informed of the agency relationships available. Rule 4.3(A) requires this written disclosure before an agreement for representation is entered into. The requirement covers transactions under Miss. Code §73-35-3 generally, with a Rule 4.4 exception for corporate, LLC, trust, and governmental parties.

questions.question_text: A Mississippi broker agrees to represent both the buyer and the seller in a transaction with written consent. What type of agency is this?
distractors: Transaction brokerage
Designated agency
Implied agency
questions.explanation: Disclosed dual agency occurs when a broker represents both the buyer and seller in the same transaction with the informed written consent of both parties. Mississippi recognizes seller's agents, buyer's agents, disclosed dual agents, and subagents. It does not recognize 'transaction brokerage' or 'designated agency.'

questions.question_text: Which agency relationship is NOT recognized under Mississippi real estate law?
distractors: Seller's agency
Buyer's agency
Disclosed dual agency
questions.explanation: Mississippi recognizes seller's agents, buyer's agents, disclosed dual agents, and subagents. It does not recognize 'transaction brokerage' or 'designated agency,' which are used in some other states. A party a licensee does not represent is simply a 'customer.'

questions.question_text: A single Mississippi brokerage ends up representing both the buyer and the seller in the same transaction. How is this handled?
questions.correct_answer: All the firm's licensees become disclosed dual agents
distractors: The firm assigns designated agents to each side
One agent becomes a transaction broker
The firm must resign from one side
questions.explanation: Mississippi does not recognize 'designated agency.' When one brokerage represents both the buyer and the seller, all of the firm's licensees become disclosed dual agents, which requires the informed written consent of both parties on MREC Form A and the MREC Dual Agency Confirmation Form.

questions.question_text: A buyer declines to sign a buyer agency agreement and works with the seller's listing agent. Under Mississippi law, how is that buyer classified?
questions.correct_answer: A customer who is not represented
distractors: A transaction broker's client
A designated agency client
The listing agent's subagent
questions.explanation: Mississippi does not recognize 'transaction brokers.' A party the licensee does not represent is a 'customer,' owed honesty and fair dealing but not the fiduciary duties of an agency relationship. The seller's agent can still show the buyer property while representing only the seller.

questions.question_text: Under Mississippi law, when must Form A be presented to a prospective client?
questions.correct_answer: Before an agreement for representation is entered into
distractors: At the closing of the transaction
Within 48 hours of first contact
Only after a written offer is prepared
questions.explanation: MREC Rule 4.3(A) requires written disclosure on an MREC Agency Disclosure Form 'before the time an agreement for representation is entered into' with the party the broker represents. For a party the broker does NOT represent, Rule 4.3(B) requires the written disclosure at the first substantive meeting. Mississippi's rules contain no 'earliest practicable opportunity' standard.

questions.question_text: A seller calls a listing agent to schedule a showing for a friend who might want to buy. Must the agent present Form A to the friend?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, at or before the first substantive meeting
distractors: No, only to the seller who called
Only if the friend signs a contract
Only after the friend makes an offer
questions.explanation: Showing property to a prospective buyer triggers the 'first substantive meeting' (Rule 4.2(H)(1)(a)), at which the listing agent must give written disclosure that the agent represents the seller (Rule 4.3(B)). The friend is an unrepresented prospective buyer — a customer — and must receive the disclosure before or just immediately prior to the showing, regardless of how the showing was arranged.

questions.question_text: An agent meets a prospective buyer at an open house. The buyer asks general questions about the neighborhood. When should Form A be presented?
questions.correct_answer: Before eliciting confidential information from the buyer
distractors: Immediately, as soon as the buyer walks in
Within 72 hours of the open house
Only after the buyer makes an offer
questions.explanation: A bona fide open house showing, preliminary 'small talk,' and answering general factual questions are expressly excluded from the 'first substantive meeting' (Rule 4.2(H)(2)). Written disclosure becomes required before eliciting confidential information about the buyer's real estate needs, motivation, or financial qualifications, or before the execution of any agreement — whichever comes first.

questions.question_text: Which of the following parties is exempt from Mississippi's agency disclosure requirements?
questions.correct_answer: Corporations, LLCs, trusts, and government entities
distractors: Buyers using a mortgage lender
Sellers listing for-sale-by-owner
Parties already represented by counsel
questions.explanation: MREC Rule 4.4 exempts licensees from the agency disclosure requirements when engaged in transactions with corporations, non-profit and professional corporations, LLCs, partnerships, REITs, business/charitable/family trusts, or governmental entities (though the common law of agency still applies). There is no exemption for financed buyers, FSBO sellers, or parties represented by counsel.

questions.question_text: A licensee delays presenting Form A until after a purchase agreement is signed. What is the likely consequence?
questions.correct_answer: A violation of Mississippi disclosure rules
distractors: No consequence if both parties agree
The contract becomes automatically void
A mandatory 30-day license suspension
questions.explanation: The first substantive meeting must occur no later than the execution of any agreement governed by Miss. Code §73-35-3 (Rule 4.2(H)), and disclosure to a represented party must precede the representation agreement (Rule 4.3(A)). Presenting Form A only after a purchase agreement is signed therefore violates MREC disclosure rules and exposes the licensee to discipline.

questions.question_text: A licensed agent is purchasing a home for personal use. What disclosure is required?
distractors: Verbal disclosure to the seller only
No disclosure if not acting as agent
Disclosure only if using MLS listings
questions.explanation: Under MREC Rule 3.3(D), licensees must disclose their licensed status on all real estate contracts in which they have an ownership interest, and in all advertising when buying or selling their own unlisted property. The duty applies even when the licensee acts purely as a principal rather than as anyone's agent.

questions.question_text: A broker sells her own investment property to an unrepresented buyer. To whom must she disclose her license?
distractors: Only the buyer's lender
Only MREC upon request
Only her responsible broker
questions.explanation: Rule 3.3(D) requires the licensed-status disclosure on the real estate contract itself — which every party to the transaction signs — and in all advertising of the licensee's own unlisted property. Disclosure only to a lender, to MREC, or to the licensee's own broker does not satisfy the rule.

questions.question_text: Which method of disclosure satisfies Mississippi's requirement when a licensee is a principal in a transaction?
distractors: Verbal disclosure at closing only
Posting notice on the MLS listing
Filing a form with the county clerk
questions.explanation: Rule 3.3(D) requires written disclosure: the licensee's status must appear on all real estate contracts in which they have an ownership interest, and in all forms of advertising for the licensee's own unlisted property. Verbal disclosure at closing or an MLS notation alone does not satisfy this written, on-the-contract requirement.

questions.question_text: A salesperson buys a rental property through another agent at a different brokerage. Must the salesperson disclose her license?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, in writing to all parties
distractors: No, the other agent handles disclosure
Only if the seller asks directly
Only if buying in her own name
questions.explanation: A licensee who is a principal must disclose their licensed status in writing to all parties regardless of whether they are using a different brokerage. The obligation belongs to the licensee-principal, not the representing agent.

questions.question_text: A licensee inherits a property and decides to sell it without using an agent. Which statement is correct?
questions.correct_answer: Must disclose licensed status in writing
distractors: No disclosure needed for inherited property
Only needs verbal disclosure to buyers
Disclosure required only if listing on MLS
questions.explanation: Regardless of how the property was acquired, a licensee selling their own property must disclose their licensed status in writing to all parties. The inheritance does not exempt the licensee from this requirement.
